2.3: he apostasia prōton kai apokaluphthē ho anthropos tes anomias ho huios tes apōleias (hē apostasia prōton kai apokaluphthē ho anthrōpos tēs anomias ho huios tēs apōleias): 'the rebellion (apostasia) comes first, and the man of lawlessness, the son of destruction, is revealed.' Apostasia — in LXX political rebellion and religious apostasy are both covered by this word; whether Paul means a final cosmic falling-away from faith or a political rebellion is debated. Ho huios tes apōleias (son of destruction) is the same phrase used of Judas in John 17:12 — linking the ultimate opponent to the prototypical betrayer. Apokaluphthē (is revealed): the passive suggests God controls even the timing of the opponent's disclosure.
2.6: to katechon / ho katechon: 'what is restraining / he who restrains' — the most debated phrase in 2 Thessalonians. The neuter (to katechon, v. 6) and masculine (ho katechon, v. 7) alternate, suggesting either a power/principle and a personal restrainer, or a rhetorical variation for the same referent. Major interpretive proposals: (1) the Roman empire/emperor (the power that prevents chaos — Tertullian, Chrysostom); (2) Paul's own missionary preaching that must be completed first; (3) the Holy Spirit who restrains evil until the end; (4) a divine decree or angel. The deliberate vagueness may be security-conscious rhetoric — naming the restrainer directly in a letter could be politically dangerous.
2.1: Erōtōmen de hymas adelphoi hyper tēs parousias tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou kai hēmōn episynagōgēs ep' auton: 'Now concerning the coming (parousias) of our Lord Jesus Christ and our being gathered together (episynagōgēs) to him.' Parousia (royal presence, coming of a dignitary) and episynagōgē (the gathering together) — the eschatological assembly of the redeemed at Christ's coming. Episynagōgē appears in Heb 10:25 (not neglecting to meet together) and echoes the OT gathering of Israel (Ps 106:47; Isa 27:12-13; Matt 24:31: he will send his angels and they will gather his elect).
2.2: eis to mē tacheōs saleuthenai hymas apo tou noos mēde throeisthai mēte dia pneumatos mēte dia logou mēte di' epistolēs hōs di' hēmōn hōs hoti enestēken hē hēmera tou kyriou: 'not to be quickly shaken (saleuthenai) in mind or alarmed, either by a spirit or a spoken word, or a letter seeming to be from us, to the effect that the day of the Lord has come.' Saleuthenai (to be shaken, as by an earthquake) and throeisthai (to be alarmed, troubled with a continuous noise) are the two verbs of disruption. Three sources of false eschatological information are listed: a prophetic utterance (pneumatos), a spoken word (logou), or a letter supposedly from Paul (epistolēs hōs di' hēmōn). The warning against a forged letter explains the autograph in 3:17.
2.4: ho antikeimenos kai hyperairōmenos epi panta legomenon theon ē sebasma hōste auton eis ton naon tou theou kathisai apodeiknynta heauton hoti estin theos: 'who opposes and exalts himself against every so-called god or object of worship, so that he takes his seat in the temple of God, proclaiming himself to be God.' The self-exaltation (hyperairōmenos — to raise oneself above, used of proud self-elevation) echoes Dan 11:36 (the king shall exalt himself and magnify himself above every god) and Ezek 28:2 (your heart is proud and you have said I am a god). The temple-sitting claim to divinity has historical resonances: Antiochus IV Epiphanes desecrated the Jerusalem temple (1 Macc 1:54); Caligula attempted to install his statue there (Philo, Legatio; Josephus, Ant. 18.261).
2.5: Ou mnēmoneuete hoti eti ōn pros hymas tauta elegon hymin: 'Do you not remember that when I was still with you I told you these things?' The reference to prior oral teaching (not preserved in any letter) explains the compression and allusiveness of the eschatological schema Paul now references. The Thessalonians had been told this; Paul only reminds them of a framework already established. This is why 2 Thessalonians is difficult to interpret without the missing oral background.
2.7: to gar mystērion ēdē energeitai tēs anomias monon ho katechōn arti heōs ek mesou genētai: 'For the mystery of lawlessness (mystērion tēs anomias) is already at work; only he who now restrains (ho katechōn) it will do so until he is out of the way.' Mystērion tēs anomias (the mystery of lawlessness) — the hidden power of lawlessness already operative, whose full disclosure awaits the removal of the restrainer. Ho katechōn (the one restraining, the restrainer — masculine participle) and to katechon (v.6, neuter — the restraining thing) — the shift from neuter to masculine is unexplained and has generated centuries of debate: candidates include the Roman empire and emperor, the preaching of the gospel, the Holy Spirit, the archangel Michael, or Paul's own apostolic ministry. The plain sense is that something/someone actively restrains the full revelation of the man of lawlessness.
2.8: kai tote apokalyphthēsetai ho anomos hon ho kyrios [Iēsous] anelei tō pneumati tou stomatos autou kai katargēsei tē epiphaneia tēs parousias autou: 'And then the lawless one (ho anomos) will be revealed, whom the Lord Jesus will kill (anelei) with the breath of his mouth (pneumati tou stomatos autou) and bring to nothing by the appearing of his coming (epiphaneia tēs parousias autou).' Pneumati tou stomatos (breath/Spirit of his mouth) echoes Isa 11:4 (he shall strike the earth with the rod of his mouth and with the breath of his lips he shall kill the wicked). Epiphaneia tēs parousias (the appearing of his coming) — combining two Greek terms for royal arrival: epiphaneia (the brilliant manifestation) and parousia (the royal presence).
2.9: hou estin hē parousia kat' energeian tou Satana en pasē dynamei kai sēmeiois kai terasin pseudous: 'The coming (parousia) of the lawless one is by the activity (energeian) of Satan with all power and false signs and wonders (sēmeiois kai terasin pseudous).' The man of lawlessness has his own parousia — a deliberate parody of Christ's parousia. The false signs and wonders (sēmeia kai terata pseudous) parody the genuine signs and wonders of the authentic apostolic mission (2 Cor 12:12; Heb 2:4). The Satanic energy (energeian tou Satana) stands against the divine energeia of v.11.
2.10: kai en pasē apatē adikias tois apollymenois anth' hōn tēn agapēn tēs alētheias ouk edexanto eis to sōthēnai autous: 'and with all wicked deception for those who are perishing, because they refused to love the truth (agapēn tēs alētheias) and so be saved.' Agatē adikias (deception of unrighteousness) is the instrument of judgment on those perishing. The crucial phrase: tēn agapēn tēs alētheias ouk edexanto — they did not receive the love of the truth. The perdition is not arbitrary but consequential: they refused what was offered.
2.11: kai dia touto pempei autois ho theos energeian planēs eis to pisteusai autous tō pseudei: 'Therefore God sends them a strong delusion (energeian planēs), so that they may believe what is false.' Energeia planēs (energetic delusion, the working of error) — God's judicial act of confirming the unbelievers in their choice. The OT precedent: God hardened Pharaoh's heart (Exod 4:21; 7:3; 10:1); Isaiah spoke God's hardening of Israel (Isa 6:9-10). The delusion is not arbitrary but the judicial ratification of the prior rejection of truth (v.10).
2.12: hina krithōsin pantes hoi mē pisteusantes tē alētheia alla eudokēsantes tē adikia: 'in order that all may be condemned who did not believe the truth but had pleasure in (eudokēsantes) unrighteousness.' The two-part description of the condemned: they did not believe the truth (a cognitive/volitional failure) and they took pleasure in unrighteousness (a moral/affective orientation). The eudokia (pleasure, delight) they chose was in wickedness rather than in God's own eudokia (good pleasure, v.11 of ch.1: eudokian agathōsynēs).
2.13: Hēmeis de opheilomen eucharistein tō theō pantote peri hymōn adelphoi ēgapēmenoi hypo kyriou hoti heilato hymas ho theos ap' archēs eis sōtērian en hagiasmō pneumatos kai pistei alētheias: 'But we ought always to give thanks to God for you, brothers beloved by the Lord, because God chose you as the firstfruits (ap' archēs — from the beginning; some MSS read aparchēn — firstfruits) to be saved, through sanctification by the Spirit and belief in truth.' The election formula: heilato hymas ho theos (God chose you). En hagiasmō pneumatos (in sanctification of the Spirit) — the Spirit's work of setting apart, which is the mechanism of election's application. Pistei alētheias (faith/belief in truth) stands against the pseudei (the false/the lie) of v.11.
2.14: eis ho ekalesen hymas dia tou euangeliou hēmōn eis peripoiēsin doxēs tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou: 'To this he called you through our gospel, so that you may obtain the glory (peripoiēsin doxēs) of our Lord Jesus Christ.' Peripoiēsis (obtaining, securing, acquisition) — the prize secured for the elect. Doxēs tou kyriou Iēsou Christou: the glory of Christ (the Shekinah-glory of the exalted Lord) is what the called receive. This echoes Rom 8:30: those he called he also justified, and those he justified he also glorified.
2.15: Ara oun adelphoi stēkete kai krateite tas paradoseis has edidachthēte eite dia logou eite di' epistolēs hēmōn: 'So then, brothers, stand firm (stēkete) and hold to the traditions (paradoseis) that you were taught by us, either by our spoken word or by our letter.' Paradoseis (traditions — from paradidōmi: to hand over, to transmit) is the technical term for transmitted authoritative teaching. In rabbinic Judaism, the oral Torah was transmitted as qabbalah (that which is received); Paul uses the same transmission-reception language (1 Cor 15:3: I delivered to you as of first importance what I also received). The double source — oral word and letter — establishes the complementary authority of both.
2.16: Autos de ho kyrios hēmōn Iēsous Christos kai [ho] theos ho patēr hēmōn ho agapēsas hēmas kai dous paraklēsin aiōnian kai elpida agathēn en chariti: 'Now may our Lord Jesus Christ himself, and God our Father, who loved us and gave us eternal comfort (paraklēsin aiōnian) and good hope through grace.' The benediction inverts the normal order (Father then Son) to Christ then Father — an unusual christological emphasis. Paraklēsis aiōnia (eternal encouragement/comfort) contrasts with the temporary comfort of earthly sources; the eschatological good hope (elpis agathē) is the hope of the glory described in v.14.
2.17: parakalesai hymōn tas kardias kai stērixai en panti ergō kai logō agathō: 'comfort your hearts and establish them in every good work and word.' Stērixai (to establish, to make firm, to set solid) — the same verb Paul used for his desire to visit the Thessalonians (1 Thess 3:2: to establish and exhort you in your faith). The divine stabilizing work is the eschatological anchor against the shaking (saleuthenai) of v.2.

1.1: Paulos kai Silouanos kai Timotheos tē ekklēsia Thessalonikeon en theō patri hēmōn kai kyriō Iēsou Christō: the same triple authorship as 1 Thessalonians (Paul, Silvanus/Silas, Timothy). The Thessalonian congregation receives its second letter within months or at most a year of the first, apparently because the eschatological confusion addressed in ch.2 has not been resolved by the first letter alone. The unusual double use of en (in God our Father and in the Lord Jesus Christ) parallels 1 Thess 1:1 but is unique in the Pauline corpus.
1.2: charis hymin kai eirēnē apo theou patros [hēmōn] kai kyriou Iēsou Christou: the standard Pauline grace-and-peace greeting. The expansion hēmōn (our) with Father makes the divine fatherhood a shared possession of apostles and congregation alike — a notable intimacy.
1.3: Eucharistein opheilomen pantote peri hymōn adelphoi kathōs axion estin hoti hyperauxanei hē pistis hymōn kai pleonazei hē agapē henos hekastou pantōn hymōn eis allēlous: 'We ought always to give thanks for you, brothers, as is right, because your faith is growing abundantly (hyperauxanei) and the love of every one of you for one another is increasing (pleonazei).' Hyperauxanei (growing beyond measure, growing super-abundantly) — the hyper-compound intensifies normal growth to overflow. The letter's thanksgiving period identifies the two developments justifying the thanksgiving: growing faith and multiplying love.
1.4: hōste autous hēmas en hymin enkauchasthai en tais ekklēsiais tou theou hyper tēs hypomonēs hymōn kai pisteōs en pasin tois diōgmois hymōn kai tais thlipsesin hais anechesthe: 'We ourselves boast about you in the churches of God for your steadfastness (hypomonē) and faith in all your persecutions and in the afflictions that you are enduring.' Hypomonē (steadfast endurance, patient standing-under) is the Christian virtue of holding ground under pressure — not passive resignation but active persistence. The Thessalonian persecutions were real social and possibly legal pressures (1 Thess 2:14-15; Acts 17:5-9).
1.5: endeigma tēs dikaias kriseōs tou theou eis to kataxiōthēnai hymas tēs basileias tou theou hyper hēs kai paschete: 'This is evidence (endeigma) of the righteous judgment (dikaias kriseōs) of God, that you may be considered worthy of the kingdom of God, for which you are also suffering.' The suffering itself is evidence of the judgment's righteousness — not proof that God has abandoned them but proof of their refinement as genuine members of the coming kingdom. The kingdom for which they suffer is the eschatological reign of God.
1.6: eiper dikaion para theō antapodounai tois thlibousin hymas thlipsin: 'since indeed it is just (dikaion) with God to repay (antapodounai) with affliction those who afflict you' — the divine lex talionis applied eschatologically. Antapodounai (to give back in return, to repay in full) reflects the OT retributive principle: the measure given will be returned (Isa 66:6; Jer 51:56). God's righteousness demands recompense.
1.7: kai hymin tois thlibomenois anesin meth' hēmōn en tē apokalypsei tou kyriou Iēsou ap' ouranou met' angelōn dynameōs autou en pyri phlogos: 'and to grant rest (anesin) with us to you who are afflicted, when the Lord Jesus is revealed (apokalypsei) from heaven with his mighty angels in flaming fire.' Apokalypsis (unveiling, revelation) — the parousia is an apokalypsis (unveiling of the hidden reality) as well as a parousia (presence/coming). En pyri phlogos (in flaming fire) echoes the theophanic imagery of Isa 66:15 (YHWH coming with fire); the divine fire-appearance is now the coming of the Lord Jesus.
1.8: didontos ekdikēsin tois mē eidosin theon kai tois mē hypakouousin tō euangeliō tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou: 'inflicting vengeance (ekdikēsin) on those who do not know God and on those who do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus.' Ekdikēsis (vengeance, punishment, judicial satisfaction) — the eschatological judgment as God's executive enforcement of justice. The two groups — those who do not know God (a Gentile category, Jer 10:25; Ps 79:6) and those who do not obey the gospel (a Jewish/religious category) — encompass all who have rejected the divine self-disclosure.
1.9: hoitines dikēn tisousin olethron aiōnion apo prosōpou tou kyriou kai apo tēs doxēs tēs ischyos autou: 'They will suffer the punishment (dikēn tisousin) of eternal destruction (olethron aiōnion), away from the presence (prosōpou) of the Lord and from the glory of his might.' Olethros aiōnios (eternal/age-long destruction) — not annihilation but permanent separation from the source of life and glory. The phrase apo prosōpou tou kyriou (from the face/presence of the Lord) echoes Isa 2:10, 19, 21 (hide from the terror of the LORD and the splendor of his majesty).
1.10: hotan elthē endoxasthēnai en tois hagiois autou kai thaumasthēnai en pasin tois pisteusasin: 'when he comes on that day to be glorified in his saints and to be marveled at among all who have believed.' The saints (hagioi) as the location of Christ's glory — his coming is simultaneously the revelation of who they are (v.10: glorified in his saints) and what he is. The marveling (thaumasthēnai) echoes the language of theophanic awe (Isa 66:5 LXX; Ps 68:35).
1.11: eis ho kai proseuchometha pantote peri hymōn hina hymas axiōsē tēs klēseōs ho theos hēmōn kai plērōsē pasan eudokian agathōsynēs kai ergon pisteōs en dynamei: 'To this end we always pray for you, that our God may make you worthy of his calling (klēseōs) and may fulfill every resolve for good and every work of faith by his power (dynamei).' Eudokia agathōsynēs (resolve for good, good pleasure toward goodness) — a compressed phrase meaning either the Thessalonians' own good resolve or God's good pleasure that produces their goodness. En dynamei (by power) — the eschatological power at work now in the congregation that will be fully manifested at the parousia.
1.12: hopōs endoxasthē to onoma tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou en hymin kai hymeis en autō kata tēn charin tou theou hēmōn kai kyriou Iēsou Christou: 'so that the name of our Lord Jesus may be glorified in you, and you in him, according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ.' The mutual glorification: the Lord Jesus is glorified in the congregation; the congregation is glorified in him. The Isa 66:5 background continues: the name of the Lord that was mocked will be glorified. The grace of God and the Lord Jesus Christ — the grammatical construction makes God and Jesus Christ a unified source of grace.

3.1: To loipon proseuchesthe adelphoi peri hēmōn hina ho logos tou kyriou trechē kai doxazētai kathōs kai pros hymas: 'Finally, brothers, pray for us, that the word of the Lord may speed ahead (trechē) and be honored (doxazētai), as happened among you.' Trechō (to run swiftly, to progress rapidly) echoes Ps 147:15 (his word runs swiftly). Doxazētai (to be honored, to be glorified) — the gospel's reception is described as the glorification of the Lord's word. The Thessalonians' own reception of the gospel is the model and precedent Paul cites.
3.2: kai hina rhysthōmen apo tōn atopōn kai ponērōn anthrōpōn ou gar pantōn hē pistis: 'and that we may be delivered from wicked and evil people. For not all have faith.' Atopōn (out-of-place, improper, absurd — used for those who are out of their proper moral category) and ponērōn (evil, actively harmful). The non-universality of faith (ou gar pantōn hē pistis — for faith is not of all) is a striking understatement: most people Paul encountered opposed the gospel rather than receiving it.
3.3: Pistos de estin ho kyrios hos stērixei hymas kai phylaxei apo tou ponērou: 'But the Lord is faithful (pistos). He will establish you and guard you against the evil one (tou ponērou).' The contrast is sharp: not all humans are faithful (v.2), but the Lord is. Pistos (faithful, trustworthy — from pistis) applied to the kyrios: the divine faithfulness is the ground of the congregation's stability despite human unfaithfulness. Tou ponērou — either the evil one (Satan, as in Matt 6:13) or evil in general. The parallel with the Lord's Prayer (deliver us from the evil one) is suggestive.
3.4: Pepoithamen de en kyriō eph' hymas hoti ha parangellomen [kai] poieite kai poiēsete: 'And we have confidence in the Lord about you, that you are doing and will do the things that we command.' The apostolic confidence is qualified en kyriō (in the Lord) — not confidence in the Thessalonians' natural reliability but in the Lord's faithfulness working through them. Parangellomen (we command, we charge authoritatively) — a stronger word than parakalō (I appeal/encourage), reflecting military command language.
3.5: Ho de kyrios kateuthynai hymōn tas kardias eis tēn agapēn tou theou kai eis tēn hypomonēn tou Christou: 'May the Lord direct your hearts to the love of God and to the steadfastness of Christ (hypomonēn tou Christou).' Kateuthynai (to direct straight, to guide) — the wish-prayer form. Hypomonē tou Christou (the endurance/steadfastness of Christ or endurance for/like Christ) — either the patient endurance that belongs to Christ (subjective genitive: Christ's own hypomonē modeled in Heb 12:2) or the endurance directed toward Christ's coming. The ambiguity is productive: both readings are theologically valid.
3.6: Parangellomen de hymin adelphoi en onomati tou kyriou Iēsou Christou stellesthai hymas apo pantos adelphou ataktōs peripatountos kai mē kata tēn paradosin hēn parelabosan par' hēmōn: 'Now we command you, brothers, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, to keep away (stellesthai) from any brother who is walking in idleness (ataktōs) and not in accord with the tradition they received from us.' Ataktōs (in a disorderly manner, out of rank) — from ataktos, the military term for a soldier who breaks formation. The ataktoi are not merely lazy but have abandoned their ordered place in the community. Stellesthai (to keep away from, to avoid, to lower sail of — a nautical term) — the shunning is partial, not total excommunication (v.15: treat as a brother).
3.7: Autoi gar oidate pōs dei mimeisthai hēmas hoti ouk ētaktēsamen en hymin: 'For you yourselves know how you ought to imitate us (mimeisthai), because we were not idle (ētaktēsamen) when we were with you.' The mimesis (imitation) appeal: the apostolic team modeled the ordered life they are now commanding. Ētaktēsamen (we were not disorderly/idle) — the same ataktos root negated: Paul explicitly says he did not practice the idleness he is now condemning.
3.8: oude dōrean arton ephagomen para tinos alla en kopō kai mochthō nykta kai hēmeran ergazomenoi pros to mē epibarēsai tina hymōn: 'nor did we eat anyone's bread without paying for it, but with toil (kopō) and labor (mochthō) we worked night and day, that we might not be a burden to any of you.' Kopō kai mochthō (toil and labor) — the double noun intensifies the effort. Nykta kai hēmeran (night and day) — working beyond normal hours. Paul's manual labor during his mission (1 Cor 9:6: Barnabas and I who work for our living; 1 Thess 2:9: working night and day) was a deliberate choice to avoid financial dependency that could compromise his message or his congregations.
3.9: ouch hoti ouk echomen exousian alla hina heautous typon dōmen hymin eis to mimeisthai hēmas: 'It was not because we do not have that right (exousian), but to give you in ourselves an example (typon) to imitate.' Exousia (authority, right) — Paul had the apostolic right to financial support from the congregations (1 Cor 9:3-14: do we not have the right to eat and drink? The Lord commanded that those who proclaim the gospel should get their living by the gospel). He waived it to set a pattern (typos) — a visible model to be replicated.
3.10: kai gar hote ēmen pros hymas touto parēngelomen hymin hoti ei tis ou thelei ergazesthai mēde esthiō: 'For even when we were with you, we would give you this command (parēngelomen): If anyone is not willing to work, let him not eat.' The si quis non vult operari nec manducet (if anyone is not willing to work, neither let him eat) was Paul's prior oral teaching, now restated in writing. The dominical echo is debated: some trace it to Jesus tradition (a logion not preserved in the Gospels); others read it as a creation-order principle (Gen 3:19). The categorical form (mēde esthiō — neither let him eat) matches the categorical form of rabbinic rules.
3.11: akouomen gar tinas peripatountas en hymin ataktōs mēden ergazomenous alla periergazomenous: 'For we hear that some among you walk in idleness, not busy at work, but busybodies (periergazomenous).' The wordplay: mēden ergazomenous alla periergazomenous (doing no work but doing over-work in others' affairs — literally working around rather than working). Periergazomenos (a busybody, one who meddles in others' business) — the word appears only here in the NT. The connection between idleness and meddlesomeness: those not occupied with their own work fill the vacuum with interference in others' affairs.
3.12: tois de toioutois parangellomen kai parakaloumen en kyriō Iēsou Christō hina meta hēsychias ergazomenoi ton heautōn arton esthiōsin: 'Now such persons we command (parangellomen) and encourage (parakaloumen) in the Lord Jesus Christ to do their work quietly (meta hēsychias) and to eat their own bread.' Meta hēsychias (with quietness, in tranquility) — the opposite of the restless meddlesomeness of v.11. The command to eat ton heautōn arton (their own bread) contrasts with eating others' bread without payment (v.8): the resolution is self-sustaining labor.
3.13: Hymeis de adelphoi mē enkakēsēte kalopoiountes: 'As for you, brothers, do not grow weary in doing good (kalopoiountes).' Enkakeō (to grow weary, to lose heart, to be cowardly in the face of difficulty) — the exhortation to the non-idle majority: the problem of the ataktoi must not cause the faithful majority to tire of doing good. Kalopoiountes (doing what is good/beautiful) is the broad category encompassing the generosity, work, and community care that the ataktoi were disrupting.
3.14: Ei de tis ouch hypakouei tō logō hēmōn dia tēs epistolēs touton sēmeiousthe mē synanamignysthai autō hina entrapē: 'If anyone does not obey what we say in this letter, take note of that person (sēmeiousthe), and have nothing to do with him (mē synanamignysthai), that he may be ashamed (entrapē).' Sēmeiousthe (mark, note down, single out) — the act of communal identification for purposes of discipline. Synanamignysthai (to mix with, to associate with) — social distancing from the unrepentant member, as in 1 Cor 5:9, 11. The goal (hina entrapē): that he may feel shame — social shame as a redemptive mechanism in an honor-shame culture.
3.15: kai mē hōs echthron hēgeisthe alla noutheteite hōs adelphon: 'Do not regard him as an enemy, but warn him as a brother.' The limit of the discipline: the shunned person remains a brother, not an enemy. Nouthetein (to warn, admonish, counsel — from nous + tithēmi: to put in mind) — the goal is restoration through frank admonition, not adversarial confrontation. This is the pastoral corrective to any punitive excess.
3.16: Autos de ho kyrios tēs eirēnēs dōē hymin tēn eirēnēn dia pantos en panti tropō. Ho kyrios meta pantōn hymōn: 'Now may the Lord of peace himself give you peace at all times in every way. The Lord be with you all.' Ho kyrios tēs eirēnēs (the Lord of peace) — peace as a divine attribute predicated of the Lord Jesus (Eph 2:14: he himself is our peace). Dia pantos en panti tropō (always in every manner/way) — the breadth of the benediction covers all circumstances. Ho kyrios meta pantōn hymōn (the Lord be with all of you) — the standard benediction of divine presence, echoing the OT Immanuel-promise (Isa 7:14; 8:8-10).
3.17: Ho aspasmos tē emē cheiri Paulou ho estin sēmeion en pasē epistolē houtōs graphō: 'I, Paul, write this greeting with my own hand. This is the sign of genuineness in every letter of mine; it is the way I write.' Sēmeion (sign, mark of authentication) — the autograph as a distinguishing mark. The phrase en pasē epistolē (in every letter) implies Paul was known to authenticate his letters this way and that a letter lacking his hand-signature should be suspected. This directly responds to the concern about forged letters in 2:2.
3.18: Hē charis tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou meta pantōn hymōn: 'The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all.' The final benediction matches 1 Thess 5:28 almost exactly. The repeated pantōn (all) — even the ataktoi who have been disciplined are included in the grace that is wished for the whole congregation. The letter ends with a broader embrace than its internal disciplinary concerns might suggest.
